top_stories.label: Top Stories
top_stories.ai_summary: The big news today: the WHO has officially declared the DR Congo–Uganda Ebola outbreak a public health emergency of international concern — the highest level of alarm — citing cross-border spread and no approved Bundibugyo vaccine. Meanwhile, President Trump rattled markets and capitals with an AI-generated 'calm before the storm' image hinting at fresh Iran strikes, even as Brent crude held above $108 with the Strait of Hormuz still essentially shut. In Gaza, mourners buried Hamas military chief al-Haddad as Israel and Hamas exchanged angry rhetoric, and the Pentagon confirmed plans to pull 5,000 troops out of Germany. The day's lighter notes: Bulgaria's Dara wins Eurovision in Vienna and centrist Nicusor Dan wins Romania's presidential runoff.

stories.headline: WHO declares Congo–Uganda Ebola outbreak a global public health emergency
stories.summary: The World Health Organization on Sunday declared the Bundibugyo strain Ebola outbreak in DR Congo and Uganda a 'public health emergency of international concern' — its highest level of alarm. As of Saturday, 80 suspected deaths, 8 lab-confirmed cases and 246 suspected cases were logged in Congo's Ituri province, with two confirmed cases (one fatal) in Kampala and one in Kinshasa. WHO called it 'extraordinary' because there is no approved vaccine or therapeutic for the Bundibugyo strain.

stories.headline: Trump posts 'calm before the storm' image as Iran war could resume within days
stories.summary: President Trump shared an AI-generated graphic of himself on a US warship in rough seas captioned 'It Was The Calm Before The Storm', alongside an animation of a US warship firing at an Iranian-flagged aircraft. The posts come amid reports the White House is finalising contingency plans for renewed strikes on Iran, with Israel and the IDF/Mossad in 'intensive' coordination. The April 17 conditional ceasefire technically expires today.

stories.headline: Strait of Hormuz still effectively shut — Brent crude holds above $108
stories.summary: Iran's blockade of the Strait of Hormuz remains in force, with tanker traffic running at roughly 5% of normal and Brent crude finishing the week at $108–$109 after an 8.1% weekly gain. The IEA warned this week that the oil market could stay severely undersupplied through October even if the fighting stops next month. The EIA now expects Brent to average $106 in May and June.

stories.headline: Hamas buries military chief al-Haddad in Gaza City; Israel braces for retaliation
stories.summary: Hundreds of Palestinians gathered Saturday at the Al-Aqsa Martyrs Mosque to bury Hamas military wing chief Izz al-Din al-Haddad — one of the last surviving architects of the October 7, 2023 attacks — alongside his wife and daughter, who were killed in Friday's Israeli airstrike on al-Rimal. Hamas confirmed his death and called it a 'treacherous' ceasefire breach. The IDF Southern Command is on heightened alert.

stories.headline: Pentagon to pull 5,000 US troops from Germany; Stryker Brigade hit first
stories.summary: The US Defense Department has announced plans to withdraw about 5,000 of the roughly 39,000 American soldiers currently stationed in Germany, with the Stryker Brigade at the Vilseck base named as the first unit affected, per Bayerischer Rundfunk. The move comes as Trump pressures NATO allies to take on more conventional defence and as the Pentagon repositions for a sustained Iran posture.

stories.headline: Pope Leo XIV: AI-directed warfare is dragging the world into a 'spiral of annihilation'
stories.summary: In his first university address as Pope, Leo XIV used a visit to Rome's La Sapienza on Thursday to condemn surging investment in AI weapons and high-tech militarisation. He named Ukraine, Gaza, Lebanon and Iran as evidence of an 'inhuman' fusion of war and technology, and called for monitoring frameworks so AI 'does not absolve humans of responsibility for their choices'. He is reportedly drafting his first encyclical on AI.

stories.headline: Russia and Ukraine resume drone war; Romania finds unexploded projectile near border
stories.summary: Ukraine reports 263 combat engagements over the last 24 hours and Russia continues nightly drone barrages after the Trump-brokered three-day Victory Day truce expired on May 12. Romania's defence ministry confirmed an unexploded 'reactive projectile' was found on private property near its Ukraine border, raising fresh NATO-spillover worries. Three civilians were killed and 63 injured in overnight strikes on Odesa and Kharkiv.

stories.headline: Trump–Xi Beijing summit ends with thin deal; markets fall sharply
stories.summary: The two-day Trump–Xi summit in Beijing wrapped Friday with a stabilisation framework, a Chinese pledge to buy 200 Boeing jets (down from a floated 500) and a commitment to import US beef and farm goods — but no breakthrough on tariffs, Taiwan or Iran. Wall Street took the underwhelming outcome poorly: the Dow fell 537 points (-1.07%) to 49,526, the S&P 500 lost 1.24% and the Nasdaq fell 1.54%.

stories.headline: Bulgaria's Dara wins Eurovision 2026 with 'Bangaranga' in Vienna
stories.summary: Bulgarian singer Dara took the 70th Eurovision Song Contest at Vienna's Wiener Stadthalle with her catchy anthem 'Bangaranga', finishing on 516 points. Israel's Noam Bettan finished a close second with 'Michelle' on the strength of a massive public vote. The final, held Saturday night, was the lighter counterweight to a week dominated by war and market turbulence.

stories.headline: Centrist Nicusor Dan defeats far-right Simion in Romania's presidential runoff
stories.summary: Bucharest mayor Nicusor Dan won Romania's presidential runoff with 53.6% on 65% turnout, beating hard-right nationalist George Simion in a result that calmed EU fears of another populist takeover on the bloc's eastern flank. Poland meanwhile heads to a June 1 runoff after nationalist historian Karol Nawrocki edged Civic Platform's Rafal Trzaskowski in the first round.

singapore.label: Singapore
singapore.ai_summary: A quiet Sunday at home: NEA's afternoon forecast points to thundery showers mainly over the north and west, so umbrellas if you're heading out. The big local stories all came in earlier this weekend — dengue cases are down 66% year-on-year but the peak season is just starting, May's first COE bidding pushed Cat B above $126,000, and MOE says no Singapore student data has yet leaked in the global Canvas LMS breach. NCID is still holding the two Hondius cruise passengers under quarantine, both still testing negative for hantavirus.

stories.headline: Sunday forecast: thundery showers mainly over north and west Singapore
stories.summary: NEA's afternoon forecast points to thundery showers mainly over northern and western Singapore in the late afternoon. Daytime temperatures will sit around 33–34°C, in line with the inter-monsoon period which is expected to bring short, heavy afternoon thunderstorms on most days through end-May. Drivers in the west should keep an eye on PUB's Telegram channel for flash-flood alerts.

stories.headline: Dengue cases down 66% so far in 2026, but peak season is just starting
stories.summary: Singapore has logged just over 600 dengue cases from January to mid-May — a 66% drop versus the same period last year, NEA said. The improvement is credited largely to the expanded Wolbachia mosquito programme. But with the traditional dengue peak of May–October now underway, the agency is urging residents to remove stagnant water at home; one dengue death has been recorded so far this year.

stories.headline: COE premiums rise across every category in May's first bidding
stories.summary: The first Certificate of Entitlement bidding round for May closed on May 6 with premiums up across the board. Category A (smaller cars) ended at $124,790, Cat B (bigger cars) at $126,236, Cat C (commercial) at $87,479 and Open (Cat E) hit a new high of $127,700. Strong dealer demand keeps new-car ownership painfully expensive heading into the second half of 2026.

stories.headline: MOE: no sensitive Singapore student data leaked yet in global Canvas hack
stories.summary: The Ministry of Education said on Friday that, so far, no sensitive information from Singapore students has been leaked in the global Canvas learning-platform breach. Hacking group ShinyHunters claimed to have stolen 3.65TB of data from 275 million users worldwide, with NUS and SIM named on affected-institution lists. The Cyber Security Agency is monitoring and supporting affected organisations.

stories.headline: Both Singapore Hondius passengers still asymptomatic at NCID
stories.summary: Both Singapore residents who were aboard the MV Hondius continue to test negative for hantavirus and remain at the National Centre for Infectious Diseases under a 30-day quarantine from their last exposure, followed by phone surveillance to day 45. CDA again reiterated there is no evidence of community transmission and the public risk remains low.

stories.headline: 18,000 more HDB flats picked for Home Improvement Programme upgrades
stories.summary: Roughly 18,000 ageing HDB flats across Singapore have been picked for upgrading works under the Home Improvement Programme. The works fix common ageing problems like leaky pipes, ceiling leaks and outdated wiring, with essential repairs fully funded by the government. Each flat's works typically take 10 working days or less once their precinct begins.

stories.headline: Parliament passes Cross-Border Railways Bill, paving way for Johor RTS Link
stories.summary: Singapore's Parliament passed the Cross-Border Railways (Border Control Co-Location) Bill, setting out how immigration and customs operations on both sides of the Causeway will work for the upcoming Johor Bahru–Singapore RTS Link. The legislation is a key step toward the RTS Link becoming operational by end-2026, expected to ease cross-border commuting for tens of thousands daily.

stories.headline: Singapore International Festival of Arts runs through May 30
stories.summary: The Singapore International Festival of Arts (SIFA) is mid-way through its 2026 run, May 15–30, with theatre, dance and visual-arts programmes across multiple venues. Boutiques Singapore: The Spring Summer Edition 2026 also runs this weekend at the F1 Pit Building. A welcome bit of weekend distraction from a tense global news cycle.

wars.label: Wars & Conflicts
wars.ai_summary: All three live theatres are on a knife edge today. Trump's 'calm before the storm' image suggests the US is genuinely weighing a fresh strike on Iran as the April ceasefire technically expires; Hormuz remains shut. In Ukraine, Russia is still bombing day and night after the Victory Day truce collapsed, and an unexploded projectile turning up on Romanian soil is a new spillover worry. In Gaza, Hamas has just buried military chief al-Haddad and vowed revenge — though sources concede they lack the rockets to follow through — while the Israel–Lebanon truce was extended 45 days but daily strikes continue on both sides.

theatres.name: Russia–Ukraine War
theatres.status: Active — Victory Day truce collapsed; record drone war resumed; first NATO-territory projectile of the month

timeline.event: Russia launches full-scale invasion of Ukraine
Russian Black Sea flagship Moskva sunk by Ukrainian Neptune missiles
Kakhovka Dam destroyed, flooding 240 sq mi; Ukraine launches failed counteroffensive
Russia takes full control of Avdiivka after months of grinding combat
Ukraine launches surprise Kursk Offensive into Russian territory
Putin and Trump hold first Russia-US summit in four years in Alaska to discuss ending the war
Trump brokers 72-hour Victory Day truce; both sides accuse the other of violations during the pause
Truce expires; Russia immediately fires 216+ drones at Ukraine including civilian infrastructure, killing 1
Russia launches 1,560+ drones over two days — biggest sustained aerial assault of the war; Ukraine retaliates with 355-drone barrage that sets Rosneft's Ryazan oil refinery ablaze, killing 4
US waiver on Russian oil purchases expires at 12:01am EDT; 3 killed and 63 injured in overnight Russian strikes on Odesa and Kharkiv; Ukrainian drone kills a civilian in Belgorod

Romania confirms an unexploded 'reactive projectile' on private property near its Ukraine border; Ukraine reports 263 combat engagements in the past 24 hours; Moldova's President Sandu warns Putin's Transnistria passport decree is a 'recruitment tool' for the war

theatres.name: US/Israel–Iran (Strait of Hormuz crisis)
theatres.status: Conditional ceasefire technically expires today; Trump posts 'calm before the storm' imagery; strait still ~5% of normal traffic

timeline.event: 12-Day War: Israel and US strike Iranian nuclear sites; ceasefire reached
Israel and US launch new joint air campaign against Iran; Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei killed in strikes
Mojtaba Khamenei installed as new Supreme Leader; Iran begins blocking Hormuz and strikes US bases and Arab states
Conditional US-Iran ceasefire declared, contingent on reopening of Hormuz
Ceasefire formally begins, set to last 30 days through May 17
Iran seizes two vessels in Hormuz after a container ship comes under fire off Oman
Trump orders US Navy to escort commercial ships through Hormuz; Iran threatens attacks
US says it intercepted Iranian attacks on three US Navy ships in the Strait
Trump rejects Iran's counter-proposal on nuclear program, calls ceasefire 'on massive life support'
Indian cargo ship carrying livestock sunk off Oman; 'unauthorised personnel' board a UAE-anchored vessel and steer it toward Iran; Trump-Xi summit ends without an Iran deal
UK confirms it has deployed drones, fighter aircraft and a Royal Navy warship to escort Hormuz shipping; Tehran says it will soon unveil a Hormuz toll mechanism

Conditional ceasefire window technically expires; Trump posts AI-generated 'calm before the storm' image and an animation of a US warship firing on an Iranian aircraft; NYT reports US/Israel finalising plans for potential renewed strikes; Brent crude holds above $108

theatres.name: Israel–Gaza / Lebanon
theatres.status: Gaza ceasefire shaken after killing of Hamas chief al-Haddad; Lebanon truce extended 45 days but daily strikes continue

timeline.event: Hamas-led attack on southern Israel kills ~1,200 and takes 250+ hostages, triggering Gaza war
Israel kills Hezbollah chief Nasrallah; pager-attack campaign and ground invasion of southern Lebanon
Israel kills Hamas Gaza commander Mohammad Sinwar; Izz al-Din al-Haddad takes over military wing
US-brokered Gaza ceasefire takes effect; hostage releases begin under Trump's Gaza plan
Israel and Lebanon agree to formal ceasefire amid wider 2026 Lebanon war
Trump announces first 3-week extension of Israel-Lebanon ceasefire
Direct Israel-Lebanon talks open in Washington; Lebanon expresses cautious optimism
Israel strikes al-Rimal in Gaza City, killing Hamas military chief Izz al-Din al-Haddad with his wife, daughter and 7 others; Israel-Lebanon ceasefire extended another 45 days

Hundreds attend al-Haddad's funeral starting at the Al-Aqsa Martyrs Mosque in Gaza City; Hamas confirms his death and calls it a 'treacherous' ceasefire breach; IDF Southern Command on heightened alert; IDF soldier killed by Hezbollah mortar in southern Lebanon

Hamas sources concede they lack the rocket capability to retaliate at scale; Israeli strikes continue in Gaza City (3 women and a child reported killed) and on Tyre outskirts despite both ceasefires nominally in force

hantavirus.label: Hantavirus Outbreak
hantavirus.ai_summary: Two big health stories collide today. The MV Hondius hantavirus outbreak is still stable at 10–11 cases and 3 deaths, with the ship now sailing toward Rotterdam for a weeks-long disinfection. But the much bigger story is WHO's declaration today of the DR Congo–Uganda Ebola Bundibugyo outbreak as a public health emergency of international concern — 80 suspected deaths and confirmed cases as far as Kampala and Kinshasa, with no approved vaccine for this strain. Both outbreaks remain low-risk for the general public outside the affected regions.

stories.headline: WHO declares DR Congo–Uganda Ebola Bundibugyo outbreak a global public health emergency
stories.summary: On May 17, WHO's Director-General formally declared the Ebola Bundibugyo outbreak in DR Congo and Uganda a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC) — its highest level of alarm. Drivers: cross-border transmission to Kampala and Kinshasa, clusters of unexplained deaths, and the absence of any approved vaccine or therapeutic for the Bundibugyo strain. WHO stressed it does not yet meet the threshold of a 'pandemic emergency'.

stories.headline: Ebola outbreak case count: 8 confirmed, 246 suspected, 80 suspected deaths
stories.summary: As of Saturday, 80 suspected deaths, 8 lab-confirmed cases and 246 suspected cases had been reported across at least three health zones in Congo's Ituri province (Bunia, Rwampara, Mongbwalu). Uganda has reported two lab-confirmed cases including one death in Kampala, and Kinshasa has logged one confirmed case in a traveller from Ituri. The high positivity rate (8 of 13 samples) suggests the true outbreak is larger than detected.

stories.headline: MV Hondius arrives in Rotterdam tonight for weeks-long medical-grade disinfection
stories.summary: After leaving Tenerife on May 11, the hantavirus-stricken Dutch expedition ship is scheduled to dock at the Port of Rotterdam on Sunday evening or Monday with 25 crew, two RIVM medics and the body of one deceased passenger on board. None of those onboard are showing symptoms. A specialist disinfection following RIVM and WHO protocols will then take several weeks before the vessel can return to service.

stories.headline: WHO holds Hondius hantavirus count at 10–11 cases with 3 deaths; risk still rated low
stories.summary: WHO's latest update lists 8 laboratory-confirmed Andes virus cases, 2 probable and 1 inconclusive, plus 3 deaths (a Dutch couple and a German woman) spread across 16 countries through cases, contacts or origin investigation. The agency still rates global public-health risk as low, but Director-General Tedros warned more cases may yet appear given the virus's 6-week incubation window as passengers return home.

stories.headline: CDC monitoring 41 Americans for hantavirus; 18 cruise passengers stay asymptomatic in Nebraska
stories.summary: CDC said on May 14 that 41 people across the United States are under monitoring, including the 18 former Hondius passengers housed at the University of Nebraska Medical Center's National Quarantine Unit in Omaha — the only federally funded quarantine facility in the country. A previously 'confirmed' US case has been downgraded to inconclusive after retesting, and CDC says there are no confirmed Andes virus cases on US soil. High-risk contacts must stay home through the full 42-day incubation window.

stories.headline: ECDC keeps Europe risk 'very low' as genomic data confirms single Hondius source
stories.summary: ECDC's May 16 threat assessment reiterates that the risk to the general European population is very low and widespread transmission is not expected. Fresh genetic sequencing strongly links all confirmed Hondius cases to a single original source of infection — most likely environmental exposure of the index passenger in Argentina before boarding — and confirms the strain is not a new variant.

stories.headline: Singapore's two Hondius residents test negative, complete isolation at NCID
stories.summary: Both Singapore residents who were aboard the MV Hondius have tested negative for hantavirus and are being held at the National Centre for Infectious Diseases for a 30-day quarantine from their last date of exposure, followed by phone surveillance to day 45. The Communicable Diseases Agency reiterated that there is no evidence of community transmission in Singapore and the public risk remains low.

stories.headline: Mpox and H5N1 outbreaks continue in the background
stories.summary: Africa CDC reports more than 9,000 mpox cases (1,480 lab-confirmed) and 53 deaths across 22 African Union countries so far in 2026, with DRC, Madagascar, Guinea, Liberia and Ghana driving most new cases. WHO has logged 13 H5N1/swine flu human cases globally in Q1 2026, including a fatal H5N1 case in a Bangladeshi child and three cases in Cambodia. Neither pathogen is showing sustained human-to-human spread and global risk assessments stay low.

cybersecurity.label: CyberSecurity
cybersecurity.ai_summary: Active exploitation dominates this cycle. Cisco's CVSS 10.0 SD-WAN auth-bypass (CVE-2026-20182) hits its CISA federal patch deadline today, linked to threat cluster UAT-8616. Microsoft Exchange zero-day CVE-2026-42897 is being exploited in the wild against on-prem servers, and a fresh anonymous disclosure pair — BitLocker bypass 'YellowKey' and CTFMON privilege-escalation 'GreenPlasma' — adds new Windows risk. ShinyHunters' 275M-record Canvas extortion ended with Instructure paying the ransom, and the strategic story remains Google's confirmation of the first AI-developed zero-day caught in the wild — LLM-assisted exploit-writing has crossed from theory into operational use.

stories.headline: CISA federal deadline today for Cisco SD-WAN auth-bypass exploited by UAT-8616
stories.summary: CISA's May 17 patch deadline for federal agencies expires today for the maximum-severity Cisco Catalyst SD-WAN Controller/Manager authentication bypass. Active exploitation has been attributed with high confidence to threat cluster UAT-8616, which abuses the vdaemon DTLS service (UDP 12346) to inject an attacker-controlled SSH key into the vmanage-admin account and then log in over NETCONF. Patch immediately.

stories.headline: Microsoft Exchange Server zero-day CVE-2026-42897 exploited in the wild
stories.summary: Microsoft disclosed an actively exploited spoofing/XSS zero-day affecting Exchange Server Subscription Edition, 2016 and 2019. Attackers send a specially crafted email that, when opened in Outlook Web Access, executes arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser context — enabling credential theft and lateral movement against on-prem mail. Apply the out-of-band fix as soon as it ships.

stories.headline: Windows BitLocker bypass 'YellowKey' and CTFMON LPE 'GreenPlasma' disclosed
stories.summary: An anonymous researcher dropped a pair of Windows zero-days: 'YellowKey' allows an attacker with physical access to bypass BitLocker on protected drives (PoC public), and 'GreenPlasma' is a local privilege escalation in the Windows Collaborative Translation Framework (CTFMON). Microsoft is investigating; in the meantime, enterprises should rotate recovery keys and audit physical access controls.

stories.headline: Microsoft May Patch Tuesday fixes wormable Netlogon RCE and DNS Client RCE
stories.summary: Microsoft's May 2026 Patch Tuesday addressed roughly 120–138 flaws, headlined by CVE-2026-41089 — an unauthenticated stack buffer overflow in Windows Netlogon that lets an attacker execute code on a domain controller with no user interaction — and CVE-2026-41096, a heap overflow in the Windows DNS Client triggered by a crafted DNS response. Both are wormable, both pose enterprise-wide pre-auth risk. Patch DCs first.

stories.headline: Google GTIG catches first AI-developed zero-day in the wild — a 2FA bypass
stories.summary: Google's Threat Intelligence Group says it disrupted an unknown actor using an LLM-generated Python exploit that bypassed two-factor authentication on a popular open-source sysadmin tool. The script bore telltale LLM fingerprints — including a hallucinated CVSS score and textbook-Pythonic style — and exploited a hard-coded trust assumption, the kind of semantic logic flaw LLMs excel at finding. Google says Gemini was not used.

stories.headline: 'Fragnesia' / 'Dirty Frag' Linux kernel LPE grants root on all major distros
stories.summary: A new local privilege-escalation flaw in the Linux kernel's XFRM ESP-in-TCP subsystem lets an unprivileged user corrupt cached pages of read-only setuid files (like /usr/bin/su) to gain root, with a working PoC already public. It is an unintended side effect of the original Dirty Frag fix and affects kernels released before May 13, 2026 across AlmaLinux, Amazon Linux, Debian, Red Hat, SUSE and Ubuntu.

stories.headline: Instructure pays ShinyHunters ransom after 275M-record Canvas LMS breach
stories.summary: Instructure reached an agreement with ShinyHunters on May 11 — one day before the leak deadline — paying a ransom to stop publication of 3.65TB of Canvas data covering ~275 million students, teachers and staff across 8,809 institutions. The exposure stemmed from a Free-For-Teacher Canvas flaw and included names, institutional emails, student IDs and inbox messages; a second wave on May 7 defaced login portals at ~330 schools.

stories.headline: node-ipc npm package backdoored with credential-stealer in supply-chain attack
stories.summary: Three malicious versions of node-ipc (9.1.6, 9.2.3, 12.0.1) — a foundational Node.js IPC library with 10M+ weekly downloads — were published to npm on May 14 by a newly-added maintainer account. Each carried an identical 80KB obfuscated credential-stealing payload. The incident lands days after the TanStack 'Mini Shai-Hulud' worm compromised 42 packages and rippled into Mistral AI, UiPath and OpenAI dev environments.

stories.headline: 'Bleeding Llama' flaw in Ollama exposes ~300,000 AI servers to memory leaks
stories.summary: A heap out-of-bounds read in Ollama's GGUF model loader lets an unauthenticated remote attacker leak the server's entire process memory using just three API calls to /api/create and /api/push — exposing environment variables, API keys, system prompts and concurrent user conversations. Researchers identified roughly 300,000 internet-exposed servers; the issue is fixed in Ollama 0.17.1.

stories.headline: Pwn2Own Berlin 2026 closes with $908K paid for 39 zero-days; Exchange falls for $200K
stories.summary: Pwn2Own Berlin 2026 paid out over $908,000 across 39 unique zero-days in two days. The standout was Orange Tsai (DEVCORE) earning $200,000 for chaining three bugs into SYSTEM-level RCE on Microsoft Exchange; Windows 11 was popped four times and AI platforms LiteLLM, NVIDIA Megatron Bridge, OpenAI Codex, Chroma and LM Studio all fell. The event hit capacity for the first time in 19 years.
